challenges.title: Salary Cap

challenges.title: Top Earner
challenges.description: Return one row: highest-paid employee from `employees` with columns `name`, `salary`.
challenges.hint: Sort descending then limit to 1.

solution.code:
SELECT name, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 1

challenges.title: Dept Rollup
challenges.description: Using `employees(department, salary)`, return each department with average salary as `avg_salary`, ordered highest first.
challenges.hint: Use AVG with GROUP BY then ORDER BY avg_salary DESC.

solution.code:
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC

challenges.title: Count Hires
challenges.description: Using `employees`, return total row count as `employee_count`. Output must be one row.
challenges.hint: COUNT(*) counts all rows.

solution.code:

FROM employees

challenges.title: Recent Orders
challenges.description: Using `orders(created_at)`, return all rows on or after `2024-01-01`, newest first.
challenges.hint: Filter with >= and sort by created_at DESC.

solution.code:
SELECT *
FROM orders
WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01'
ORDER BY created_at DESC

challenges.title: Distinct Cities
challenges.description: Using `customers(city)`, return unique non-null cities sorted alphabetically.
challenges.hint: Combine DISTINCT, IS NOT NULL, and ORDER BY.

solution.code:
SELECT DISTINCT city
FROM customers
WHERE city IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY city

challenges.title: Price Window
challenges.description: Using `products(name, price)`, return rows where price is between 10 and 50 inclusive, sorted by price ascending.
challenges.hint: BETWEEN 10 AND 50 is inclusive.

solution.code:
SELECT name, price
FROM products
WHERE price BETWEEN 10 AND 50
ORDER BY price ASC

challenges.title: Customer Totals
challenges.description: Using `orders(customer_id, amount)`, return each customer with total spent as `total_spent`, sorted descending.
challenges.hint: SUM + GROUP BY + ORDER BY total_spent DESC.

solution.code:
SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
ORDER BY total_spent DESC

challenges.title: Join Names
challenges.description: Using `orders(id, customer_id)` and `customers(id, name)`, return `order_id` and customer `name` for each order.
challenges.hint: Join orders.customer_id to customers.id.

solution.code:
SELECT o.id AS order_id, c.name
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id

challenges.title: No Orders
challenges.description: Using `customers(id, name)` and `orders(customer_id)`, return customers with no orders. Output: `id`, `name`.
challenges.hint: LEFT JOIN then keep rows where order side is NULL.

solution.code:
SELECT c.id, c.name
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.customer_id IS NULL

challenges.title: Daily Revenue
challenges.description: Using `orders(created_at, amount)`, return `order_day` and `daily_total`, grouped by day and sorted ascending by day.
challenges.hint: Extract DATE(created_at), then SUM and GROUP BY.

solution.code:
SELECT DATE(created_at) AS order_day, SUM(amount) AS daily_total
FROM orders
GROUP BY DATE(created_at)
ORDER BY order_day ASC

challenges.title: Second Highest
challenges.description: Using `employees(name, salary)`, return all employees whose salary equals the second highest distinct salary.
challenges.hint: Find second distinct salary in subquery, then filter by it.

solution.code:
SELECT name, salary
FROM employees
WHERE salary = (
  SELECT DISTINCT salary
  FROM employees
  ORDER BY salary DESC
  LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1
)

challenges.title: Running Balance
challenges.description: Using `transactions(id, created_at, amount)`, return each row plus running total `running_total` ordered by `created_at`.
challenges.hint: Use SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY created_at).

solution.code:
SELECT
  id,
  created_at,
  amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY created_at) AS running_total
FROM transactions
ORDER BY created_at

challenges.title: Latest Per Customer
challenges.description: Using `orders(id, customer_id, created_at)`, return each customer's latest order as `customer_id`, `order_id`, `created_at`.
challenges.hint: ROW_NUMBER partitioned by customer, ordered by date desc.

solution.code:
SELECT customer_id, id AS order_id, created_at
FROM (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    id,
    created_at,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY created_at DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders
) t
WHERE rn = 1

steps.title: Understand the goal

challenges.title: Gap Finder
challenges.description: Using `events(event_date)`, return rows where the gap from previous event is more than 1 day. Output: `event_date`, `previous_date`, `gap_days`.
challenges.hint: Use LAG to compare each date with previous row.

solution.code:
WITH dated AS (
  SELECT
    event_date,
    LAG(event_date) OVER (ORDER BY event_date) AS previous_date
  FROM events
)
SELECT
  event_date,
  previous_date,
  event_date - previous_date AS gap_days
FROM dated
WHERE previous_date IS NOT NULL
  AND event_date - previous_date > 1
ORDER BY event_date

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: Read the requirement carefully and focus on producing the exact expected output or return value.

steps.title: Implement the core logic

    LAG(event_date) OVER (ORDER BY event_date) AS previous_date
  FROM events
)
SELECT
  event_date,
  previous_date,
  event_date - previous_date AS gap_days
FROM dated
WHERE previous_date IS NOT NULL
  AND event_date - previous_date > 1
ORDER BY event_date

challenges.title: Median Salary
challenges.description: Using `employees(salary)`, return median salary as `median_salary`. Assume support for `PERCENTILE_CONT`.
challenges.hint: Median is percentile 0.5 in an ordered group.

solution.code:
SELECT
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) AS median_salary
FROM employees

steps.title: Understand the goal

FROM employees

challenges.title: Consecutive Streak
challenges.description: Using `logins(user_id, login_date)`, return each user's longest streak of consecutive login days as `longest_streak`.
challenges.hint: Use login_date - row_number grouping trick to identify streaks.

solution.code:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    login_date,
    login_date - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date) AS grp
  FROM logins
), grouped AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    grp,
    COUNT(*) AS streak_len
  FROM ranked
  GROUP BY user_id, grp
)
SELECT
  user_id,
  MAX(streak_len) AS longest_streak
FROM grouped
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY user_id

steps.title: Understand the goal

    grp,
    COUNT(*) AS streak_len
  FROM ranked

)
SELECT
  user_id,
  MAX(streak_len) AS longest_streak
FROM grouped
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY user_id
